✦ 2026년 5월 13일부터 — Beginning May 13, 2026
Hannah's Seoul Searching
Study Abroad Experience: Seoul, South Korea

Welcome! I am Hannah Jeongseo Kim, a first-year honors student at the University of Cincinnati, studying political science, psychology, and German. This website is a record of my seven month study abroad experience in South Korea studying at KU KLC and Korea University. 

🇰🇷 Seoul 2026 KU Korean Language Center KU Fall Exchange Political Science Korean-American Pre-Law UC Honors

나에 대해 — About Me
Hannah Jeongseo Kim
Political Science + Psychology Double Major, German Minor
  • UniversityUniversity of Cincinnati
  • YearClass of 2029 (Freshman)
  • MajorsPolitical Science & Psychology
  • MinorGerman
  • ProgramUniversity Honors — Global Citizen Scholar
  • In SeoulMay 13 – December 2026
  • LanguagesEnglish, Korean, German (in progress)
  • CareerLaw / Diplomacy / Human Rights

Who I Am

I am a rising sophomore at the University of Cincinnati. While I was born and raised in Cincinnati, OH, I have always expressed interest in traveling, especially to Korea.  My parents are both Korea University alumni and have greatly influenced my decision to participate in this exchange program. 

I am an honors student double majoring in political science and psychology, pursuing a career at the intersection of law and international diplomacy. I am constantly looking for ways to 

I presented original undergraduate research at the UC Annual Symposium on South Korean democratic resistance and radical hope, received the Tolley International Human Rights Scholarship, and am a Cincinnatus Century Scholar. Through my study abroad experience I hope to develop my research on democratic development and apply this knowledge to create more effective and peaceful international relationships and institutions.

Academic Timeline

  • Fall 2025 – Spring 2026
    Freshman year at UC. Presents at Annual Symposium. Awarded Tolley Scholarship. Nominated for KU exchange.
  • May 2026
    Departure for Seoul. Brief vacation and settling in.
  • June – August 2026
    KU Korean Language Center (KLC) Regular Program. Full time student coursework with UC.
  • September – December 2026
    KU Fall Exchange Semester. Political science & psychology coursework.
  • Spring 2027+
    Return to UC.

Interests & Research Areas

Korean Democratic Resistance Radical Hope Theory Human Rights Law Political Psychology Mental Health & Culture US-Korea Relations Comparative Democratization Cross-Cultural Psychology Pre-Law German Studies
학문적 연구 — Academic Research
Research & Scholarship
Undergraduate research at the intersection of political science, Korean studies, and behavioral psychology.
📄 Featured Paper · UC Annual Symposium 2026
South Korean Democratic Resistance and the Politics of Radical Hope
"This paper examines South Korea's pro-democracy movement of the 1980s through the theoretical lens of  'radical hope' — the capacity to hope in conditions where one's conceptual framework for understanding the future has been threatened or destroyed. Drawing on archival sources and witness testimony, I argue that Korean democratic actors employed a distinctive form of hope that was not merely optimistic but constitutively political..."
Radical Hope Korean Democracy Political Philosophy Comparative Politics 1980s Democratization

Presented at the UC Annual Symposium on Undergraduate Research, Spring 2026. Currently being expanded for submission to the Pi Sigma Alpha Undergraduate Journal of Politics (February 2027 deadline) and the Journal of Politics and International Affairs.

Future Research Directions
Developing Research Agenda

🧠 Political Psychology & Identity

How Korean-Americans navigate dual political identities and civic participation. Bridging political science and behavioral psychology through a personal lens.

🌏 Mental Health in East Asian Cultures

Stigma around mental health in collectivist societies. How Confucian values and social face concerns shape help-seeking behavior in Korea.

⚖️ Democratic Backsliding

Expanding the Radical Hope paper into a comparative framework. Application to other post-authoritarian societies beyond South Korea.

🔬 Psychology of Protest

What motivates sustained collective action versus burnout. Social identity theory applied to democratic movements.

🏛️ Human Rights & International Law

Legal frameworks governing democratic rights. North Korean human rights documentation and advocacy law.

🇩🇪 Comparative European Democracy

Germany's post-war democratic reconstruction as a comparative case. Planned for Germany study abroad junior year.

일기 — Journal
The Blog
Weekly reflections, daily observations, and honest writing from seven months in Korea.
May 14, 2026 ✈️ Arrival
도착 — I'm Here: First 24 Hours in Seoul
The moment the plane touched down at Incheon, something in me shifted. Fourteen hours from Cincinnati to the country my parents have always called home. I didn't expect to cry at customs, but here we are.

My grandmother was waiting at the arrival gate. She's 74 and she still moves faster than me through a crowd. She grabbed my largest suitcase before I could protest and started talking at a pace I struggled to follow — a reminder that the Korean I speak at home is household Korean, not the speed or register of someone who's lived here their whole life.

Incheon Airport is extraordinary. Coming from CVG, the scale is almost incomprehensible. The train from the airport to Seoul felt futuristic — smooth, fast, clean, with a digital display counting down every station in Korean, English, Japanese, and Chinese simultaneously. I practiced reading the hangul signs the whole way. I got about 70% of them.

My grandmother's apartment is in a residential neighborhood about 40 minutes from Anam. It smells like doenjang and lavender fabric softener and something unidentifiable that I now realize is simply what Korea smells like. I fell asleep at 7pm and woke at 3am. Jetlag is its own kind of brutality.

May 22, 2026 🗾 Japan
Five Perfect Days in Osaka: What Nobody Tells You
Japan is genuinely the easiest solo travel I've ever done. Osaka is the right base, Fushimi Inari at dawn is everything, and the convenience store food deserves its own post.

I flew Seoul-Osaka on Peach Aviation for ₩89,000 — about $65. Kansai Airport is one train ride from Namba, the heart of Osaka. I was at my hostel by 2pm on day one. The female-only dorm I booked had six beds, all occupied by solo female travelers from five different countries. By the end of the trip we'd all exchanged contacts.

Fushimi Inari at 6:30am: arrive before the crowds. The thousands of vermilion torii gates stretch up the mountain in a corridor that narrows as you climb. At the top, the city unfolds below you through the pines. I was mostly alone up there. It was one of those moments you can't plan — you just have to be there.

The convenience store situation. I need to say this clearly: Japanese 7-Eleven is a different category of institution from American 7-Eleven. The egg salad sandwiches. The onigiri. The hot foods counter. The seasonal drinks. I ate at least one convenience store meal every day and I do not regret a single yen of it.

Nara was the day that made me feel like a child in the best possible way. Deer that bow when you bow. A bronze Buddha the height of a building. An ancient park in the middle of a modern city. I gave half my deer crackers to a particularly pushy deer and the other half to a small child who had dropped hers. Somehow this is Korea being home and Japan being the best possible detour.

June 14, 2026 📖 KLC
KLC Orientation Day: Academic Korean is a Different Language
I grew up speaking Korean at home. I thought this would give me a significant head start. I was wrong about how different written and academic Korean is from household speech.

The Korea University Korean Language Center campus is inside KU's main campus near Anam Station — you walk through the main gate, past the red-brick buildings that have been photographed a thousand times, and find a smaller building tucked behind the library. My classroom is on the third floor with a view of the mountain.

There are thirty-two students in my level. Eight are from Japan, seven from China, four from the US (including me), and the rest from a spread of countries across Southeast Asia, Europe, and South America. We are a genuinely international room. Our teacher, 선생님, speaks only Korean from the moment class begins. This was jarring for the first twenty minutes and then somehow became normal.

The gap between spoken household Korean and academic written Korean is enormous. My reading is better than I expected. My writing is worse. My ability to conjugate formal speech levels under pressure is humbling. I knew this gap existed intellectually — actually encountering it in a classroom is different. I am a beginner in the ways that matter here, and that is uncomfortable and exciting in equal measure.

June 20, 2026 🍜 Food
What I Eat in a Week Near Korea University
A realistic food diary from Anam-dong on a student budget — from 편의점 breakfasts to late-night 라면 to the best 순두부찌개 I've had in my life.

Breakfast is almost always from the GS25 two minutes from my building. Triangle kimbap (참치마요, always) and a canned coffee that costs ₩1,200. I've stopped feeling guilty about this. The triangle kimbap of Korea is a masterpiece of engineering and flavor density.

Lunch near campus has a rhythm. The student cafeteria serves a set meal for ₩4,500 — usually a protein, two sides, soup, and rice. This is possibly the best value meal I will eat in my life. It is not always delicious but it is always filling and always costs less than a coffee back home.

The 순두부찌개 place I found on my third day in the neighborhood has become a ritual. ₩8,000 for a ceramic bowl of silken tofu in spicy red broth that arrives still boiling. You crack a raw egg into it at the table. It is simple and perfect. I have eaten there seven times in two weeks.

서울 생활 — Seoul Living
Life in Seoul
Guides, tips, and honest reflections on living as an international student in Seoul.
Korean Language Center
KLC Regular Program

The KLC Regular Program is an intensive academic Korean course running three hours per day, five days a week. Here's everything I wish I knew before arriving.

📅

Schedule

Mon–Fri, 9AM–12PM. Three hours of intensive instruction daily. 80% attendance required — missing more than 20% risks enrollment.

📊

Placement

Placed by written test and interview. Five levels from beginner to advanced. Reassessment possible after Week 2.

🏠

Housing

Frontier Hall application opens May 7 at noon KST — first come first served. Double occupancy. En-suite bathroom. No kitchen.

💰

Cost

KRW 1,215,000 per semester (with alumni discount). Frontier Hall dorm: KRW 1,400,000. Meals not included.

🌍

Classmates

Truly international cohort — Japan, China, US, Europe, Southeast Asia. Most levels have fewer than 20 students.

📝

Homework

Daily vocabulary quizzes, weekly writing assignments, one oral presentation per month. Mid-term and final exams.

Korea University Campus
Korea University
📍

Location

Anam Station, Line 6 (Orange). The main gate is a 5-minute walk from the station exit.

🏛️

Campus

Red-brick buildings and mountain backdrop. The central plaza 민주광장 is historically significant — site of pro-democracy protests.

📚

Library

CJ Library is open until 10pm. Quiet, well-resourced, excellent for study. KU ID required for entry.

🍱

Cafeteria

Student 학식 meal: KRW 4,500. Includes protein, two sides, soup, and rice. Lunch rush 12:00–12:30.

🏘️

Anam-dong

Right next to KU. Most student-heavy, cheapest food, tons of pc방 and study cafes. Functional over beautiful.

🌸

Hyehwa

One stop on Line 4. Theater district, beautiful streetscape, good cafes and bookshops. Quieter, more cultured vibe.

🎭

Hongdae

30 min from KU. Young, international, energetic. Best for nightlife, indie music, street art. Gets expensive.

🏙️

Dongdaemun

20 min by subway. 24-hour shopping, great street food, History Design Plaza worth visiting. Very local feeling.

  • 🥣
    순두부찌개 (Sundubu Jjigae)

    Silken tofu stew. Spicy, rich, arrives boiling. Crack a raw egg in at the table. Budget: ₩8,000-9,000.

  • 🍙
    편의점 삼각김밥 (Triangle Kimbap)

    The GS25 참치마요 is a daily necessity. ₩1,200. Perfect breakfast. No shame in this.

  • 🍗
    치킨 (Korean Fried Chicken)

    Delivery culture is real. BBQ Chicken, Bhc, or local spots. ₩18,000-22,000. Order with beer (치맥 culture).

  • 🫕
    부대찌개 (Budae Jjigae)

    Army stew with ramyeon, spam, sausage, kimchi. Especially good in Uijeongbu but available everywhere. ₩9,000.

  • 🥞
    호떡 (Hotteok)

    Sweet pancake filled with brown sugar, honey, seeds. Street food. ₩1,500. Do not eat when it's too hot. (I have made this mistake.)

  • 🍜
    학식 (University Cafeteria)

    Set lunch at KU: ₩4,500. The best value meal in Seoul. Varies daily. Arrive before 12:15 to avoid the rush.

💳

T-money Card

Buy at the airport or any CU/GS25. Load with ₩30,000 to start. Tap in and out on all buses and subways.

🚇

Subway

Line 6 (Orange) to Anam Station for KU. Seoul subway is extraordinary — clean, punctual, fully bilingual, air-conditioned.

🚌

Bus

Same T-money card works. Cheaper than subway for some routes. Takes more navigation skill.

🚕

Kakao Taxi

Like Uber but Korean. Download Kakao T app. Set destination in English — it works. Never stand on a street flagging — always app.

📱

SIM Card

Buy at Incheon Airport arrivals (KT or SKT). Around ₩30,000/month for unlimited data. Or set up eSIM before landing via Airalo.

💴

Cash vs. Card

Korea is increasingly card-friendly but some traditional restaurants and markets are cash only. Keep ₩50,000 cash on you.

🕐

Time Zone

KST = UTC+9 = EST+13 or EDT+14. Good for calling home on evenings (your morning here is their late afternoon).

🏥

Healthcare

Register for National Health Insurance (NHI) at immigration when you get your ARC. Clinic visits with NHI: ₩5,000-10,000.

일본 여행 — Japan Travel
Five Days in Japan
A solo budget trip from Seoul to Osaka: what I did, what I spent, and what I'd do differently.

Osaka Base · May 18–23, 2026

Solo female travel · ¥65,000 total budget ($450) · Flight, hostel, food, transport, sights

1
Tuesday

Arrive Osaka — Dotonbori & Shinsekai

Peach Aviation, ICN → KIX, 2 hours. Took the Nankai Line into Namba (35 min, ¥920). Checked into female dorm hostel in Namba area. Evening: walked Dotonbori canal — the neon, the giant crab, the takoyaki vendors. Ate at three different takoyaki stalls. Ended the night at Shinsekai for kushikatsu.

Dotonbori CanalShinsekaiTakoyaki tour
2
Wednesday

Kyoto Day Trip — Fushimi Inari & Arashiyama

JR Rapid to Kyoto (15 min, ¥560). Fushimi Inari at 7am — arrived before the crowds. The torii corridor at dawn is one of the most beautiful things I've seen. Took the mountain trail halfway up. Then to Arashiyama: bamboo grove, Tenryu-ji garden, riverside walk. Back to Osaka by evening for ramen.

Fushimi Inari (7am)Arashiyama BambooTenryu-ji Garden
3
Thursday

Hiroshima & Miyajima Island

Shinkansen to Hiroshima (90 min, ¥9,000 round trip). The Peace Memorial Museum is mandatory and devastating. The A-Bomb Dome. Then ferry to Miyajima Island for the floating torii of Itsukushima Shrine at high tide. Deer on the island like Nara but wilder. Back to Osaka by 9pm exhausted and glad I went.

Peace Memorial MuseumA-Bomb DomeMiyajima IslandItsukushima Shrine
4
Friday

Nara — Deer Park & Todai-ji Temple

Train to Nara (45 min, ¥820). The deer really do bow when you bow. I bowed at thirty deer. Every single one bowed back. Todai-ji Temple and its giant bronze Buddha is genuinely as impressive as advertised. Naramachi in the afternoon — the old merchant district, quiet and walkable and full of craft shops.

Nara Deer ParkTodai-ji TempleNaramachi
5
Saturday

Kuromon Market & Departure

Final morning at Kuromon Ichiba Market — Japan's kitchen. Oysters, tamagoyaki, wagyu skewers. Bought green tea Kit Kats and tenugui towels for gifts. Nankai Line back to Kansai Airport. Flight home to Seoul. Total trip cost including flight: approximately ₩600,000 ($430).

Kuromon MarketDotonbori (farewell)

🔒 Private — Diary
My Diary
Honest, unfiltered entries. Not for public consumption.
Thursday, May 14, 2026
Mood: overwhelmed · exhausted · grateful · a little tearful
I didn't expect to cry at customs

I don't know how to explain what it felt like handing my Korean passport to the immigration officer and hearing her speak to me in Korean — natural, fast, assuming I understood — without a pause or a question about where I was from. I understood most of it. My hands were shaking a little.

Grandmother looks exactly like I remembered and also older in ways that make my chest hurt. She grabbed my largest suitcase immediately and started talking at a pace that reminded me I speak household Korean and she lives in it. I kept up. Mostly. Good enough.

I'm writing this at 3am because of jetlag. Her apartment smells like home in a way that Cincinnati never quite has. I don't know what to do with that feeling yet.

Sunday, May 17, 2026
Mood: restless · excited · a little lonely
Japan leaves in three days and I'm nervous about traveling alone

Everyone keeps telling me solo female travel in Japan is the safest in the world. I know this. I have read the statistics. I have read the Reddit threads. I am still nervous in the particular way that only comes from doing something genuinely new.

I booked the hostel. I have the eSIM. I have a paper copy of my itinerary because my dad insisted and he was right. I think I'm more scared of the loneliness than the safety. Seven months is a long time. Japan is the first time I'll be completely alone, no family nearby, in an entirely unfamiliar country.

I think I need to go exactly to understand that I can.

June 13, 2026
Mood: humbled · determined · a little embarrassed
First day of KLC — I was overconfident

I thought my household Korean would carry me. It does not carry me in the way that matters. I know how to ask my grandmother if she wants more rice. I do not know how to conjugate an academic argument in 존댓말 under pressure with thirty people watching.

The placement test was fine. My level is appropriate. But appropriate and comfortable are different things. Everyone else in my class seems either more formally trained than me or less self-conscious about their errors. Both feel like advantages.

선생님 said my pronunciation was very good. She said it in a way that felt like a consolation prize. I will take it anyway.

🔒 Private — Notes
Notes & Lists
Miscellaneous private notes, lists, and thoughts.

📋 Korea To-Do (Ongoing)

Open Korean bank account (KEB Hana) · Register for NHI at immigration · Buy Korean SIM · Set up Kakao · Apply Frontier Hall May 7 · Send spring transcript to KU GSC · Email Asan Institute · Start GWIKS inquiry

Updated May 13, 2026

✍️ Paper Draft — Radical Hope

Working title: "Democratic Resistance as Radical Hope: The 1980s South Korean Pro-Democracy Movement." Expanding for Pi Sigma Alpha submission Feb 2027. Need to incorporate Korean-language primary sources during KLC. Key gap: comparative cases needed — only one case study currently. Consider: Spain's Transition, Poland's Solidarity movement as parallel frameworks.

Created June 1, 2026

🎓 Law School Thoughts (Private)

Georgetown (international law clinic, top IR program), GWU (Korean studies connection, GWIKS), American University (human rights law), UC Berkeley (strong international program, Korean studies). LSAT target: 168+. Need to start seriously by junior year fall. Mock Trial when I return. Keep 3.85+ GPA.

Created May 20, 2026

💭 Scholarship Applications (Fall Priority)

TRIALS Program (apply Oct 2026, due Jan 2027) · Fordham IDEAL (apply fall 2026, summer 2027) · GWIKS Research Fellows (fall 2026) · APA SUPER Fellowship (spring 2027, summer 2027) · McLean Harvard Mental Health (fall 2026 for summer 2027)
